subject: [PATCH 3/3] mm: read remote memory without the mmap lock where possible

reviews.author: Suren Baghdasaryan
reviews.summary:
The reviewer noted that using per-VMA locks in access_remote_vm_fast() could lead to inconsistent data when the read spans multiple VMAs, as neighboring VMAs can be unmapped and remapped while reading, and suggested checking if the entire read is contained within one VMA before falling back to mmap_lock.

Reviewer noted that the patchset could be simplified if CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK is removed, referencing a separate patchset by Dave Hansen that achieves this.
reviews.raw_body:
I don't think we should be using per-VMA locks if the read spans
multiple VMAs. Doing that would risk a possibility of reading
inconsistent data since we are locking one VMA at a time. While we
load and read VMA, its neighboring VMA can be unmapped and another one
can be mapped in its place. So, our read spanning both VMAs will
return inconsistent data. access_remote_vm_fast() can check if the
entire read is contained within one VMA and if not, fall back to
mmap_lock.

---

Just FYI, Dave Hansen is working on a patchset [1] that removes
CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK and makes per-VMA locks always available. This
would simplify this patchset too.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260610230409.A44D29FA@davehans-spike.ostc.intel.com/

2026-06-16.patch_summary: This patch adds an opportunistic, read-only fast path to the Linux kernel's memory access functions that allows reading remote memory without taking the mmap lock when possible. The existing slow path still uses the mmap lock for reads and writes. The new fast path checks VMA permissions and grabs a short-lived reference to a present page before copying it out. If the page is not present, or if there are other issues like hugepages or I/O mappings, it falls back to the slow path. This change aims to reduce contention on large machines by avoiding unnecessary locking for common read operations.

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Usama Arif noted that the patch should also perform FOLL_ANON check from check_vma_flags() to reject non-anonymous VMAs when FOLL_ANON is set, referencing a specific commit and CVE-2018-1120

Reviewer noted that the new resident-page fast path does not call folio_mark_accessed(), which changes reclaim behavior for pages repeatedly read through access_remote_vm().
reviews.raw_body:
This should also do the FOLL_ANON check from check_vma_flags().

check_vma_flags() rejects non-anonymous VMAs when FOLL_ANON is set:

	if ((gup_flags & FOLL_ANON) && !vma_anon)
		return -EFAULT;

That flag is used by fs/proc/base.c for /proc/PID/cmdline and
/proc/PID/environ.  It was added by commit 7f7ccc2ccc2e ("proc: do not
access cmdline nor environ from file-backed areas"), which fixed
CVE-2018-1120 by making those proc files refuse file-backed argv/env
areas.

---

__access_remote_vm() slow path calls get_user_page_vma_remote() which calls
get_user_pages_remote(). get_user_pages_remote() adds FOLL_TOUCH and then the
page-table walk eventually reaches follow_page_pte().

The new resident-page fast path copies the same data without doing an
equivalent folio_mark_accessed().

That changes reclaim behaviour for pages repeatedly read through
access_remote_vm(), such as /proc/PID/cmdline polling. I think you
should mark the folio as accessed.

2026-06-18.patch_summary: This patch adds an opportunistic, read-only fast path to the Linux kernel's memory access functions that allows reading remote memory without taking the mmap lock when possible. The existing slow path still uses the mmap lock for reads and writes. The new fast path checks VMA permissions and grabs a short-lived reference to a present page before copying it out. If the page is not present, or if there are other issues like hugepages or I/O mappings, it falls back to the slow path. This change aims to reduce contention on large machines by avoiding unnecessary locking for common read operations.
